message.content: Adversaries may exploit improperly set file permissions in executable installer files to hijack execution flow and run malicious payloads. This technique involves overwriting target binaries within installer directories, often located in the %TEMP% directory, with malicious binaries. If the original process runs with elevated privileges, the malicious code will also execute with those higher permissions, potentially including SYSTEM level. This can lead to privilege escalation and persistence if the process is scheduled to run at specific times or events, such as system bootup. Common exploitation methods include taking advantage of weak permissions in self-extracting installers and DLL Search Order Hijacking. Mitigation strategies include ensuring proper file and directory permissions, using application whitelisting, and monitoring for changes in critical directories and binaries. Regularly updating and patching software to address known vulnerabilities in installers can also reduce the risk of exploitation.
